skill.name: citation-management
skill.description: Manage academic citations by searching scholarly sources, extracting metadata, validating references, and formatting BibTeX entries.
skill.summary: Search scholarly sources and produce cleaner, validated BibTeX references.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-volume patterns, but manual review found most external command, weak cryptography, and obfuscation alerts to be false positives from Markdown examples or normal Python code. The confirmed risk is medium because the skill intentionally uses Bash-enabled scripts with outbound network requests, optional NCBI credentials, and user-selected file writes.

medium_findings.title: Outbound Requests to Scholarly Services
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally sends search queries, DOIs, PMIDs, arXiv IDs, and validation requests to external scholarly services. This is legitimate for citation management, but it can disclose research topics and identifiers to third parties.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct HTTP client usage is present in the scripts, and the destinations match the skill purpose. I did not find evidence of covert or unrelated endpoints.

medium_findings.title: Optional NCBI Credentials Sent to PubMed API
medium_findings.description: The PubMed tools read NCBI_API_KEY and NCBI_EMAIL from arguments or environment variables and include them in NCBI request parameters. This is a normal Entrez API pattern, but users should treat those values as credentials and avoid sharing logs or command lines.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential handling is explicit and tied to NCBI API calls. There is no evidence that keys are written to disk or sent to unrelated services.

medium_findings.title: Writes to User-Selected Output Files
medium_findings.description: Several scripts write formatted citations, reports, or search results to paths supplied by the caller. This is expected functionality, but it can overwrite files if a user provides an unsafe destination.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are direct and user-facing output features. I found no evidence of hidden persistence or writes outside requested output behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many static external command findings are Markdown examples showing how a user may run the included scripts. They are not Ruby backtick execution or automatic command execution by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled flagged lines are fenced Markdown commands and checklist examples. They document user-invoked commands rather than executing commands automatically.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Appear to Be False Positives
low_findings.description: Sampled weak cryptography locations reference citation text, sorting, formatting, or command examples rather than cryptographic hashing or encryption. No evidence found of weak cryptography used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain citation metadata guidance or normal formatting code. I did not find evidence that cryptographic algorithms are implemented or used.

low_findings.title: Obfuscation Heuristic Appears to Be Normal Python and Type Hints
low_findings.description: The scripts use ordinary imports, comments, type hints, lists, dictionaries, and argparse code. No prompt injection text, encoded payload, dynamic eval, or hidden execution path was found in the reviewed entry points.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static obfuscation signal is explained by normal Python syntax and library use in the sampled scripts. Broad text search found no prompt-injection phrases matching the audit warning list.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound Requests to Scholarly Services
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill intentionally sends search queries, DOIs, PMIDs, arXiv IDs, and validation requests to external scholarly services. This is legitimate for citation management, but it can disclose research topics and identifiers to third parties.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct HTTP client usage is present in the scripts, and the destinations match the skill purpose. I did not find evidence of covert or unrelated endpoints.

dangerous_patterns.title: Optional NCBI Credentials Sent to PubMed API
dangerous_patterns.description: The PubMed tools read NCBI_API_KEY and NCBI_EMAIL from arguments or environment variables and include them in NCBI request parameters. This is a normal Entrez API pattern, but users should treat those values as credentials and avoid sharing logs or command lines.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential handling is explicit and tied to NCBI API calls. There is no evidence that keys are written to disk or sent to unrelated services.

dangerous_patterns.title: Writes to User-Selected Output Files
dangerous_patterns.description: Several scripts write formatted citations, reports, or search results to paths supplied by the caller. This is expected functionality, but it can overwrite files if a user provides an unsafe destination.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are direct and user-facing output features. I found no evidence of hidden persistence or writes outside requested output behavior.

content.value_statement: Academic references are easy to mistype, duplicate, or format inconsistently. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code search sources, extract metadata, validate entries, and clean BibTeX files.
actual_capabilities: Search PubMed with date filters, publication types, result limits, and BibTeX or JSON export.
Search Google Scholar through the scholarly library with year filters and citation sorting.
Convert DOI, PMID, arXiv ID, and article URL inputs into citation metadata.
Validate BibTeX files for required fields, DOI resolution, duplicates, and formatting issues.
Format, sort, deduplicate, and rewrite BibTeX files using included Python scripts.
Provide citation checklists and reference guides for BibTeX and academic metadata workflows.
limitations: Google Scholar search depends on the scholarly package and can be rate limited or blocked.
External APIs can return incomplete metadata, stale records, or temporary errors.
NCBI API key and email values are optional but must be supplied by the user when needed.
Generated citations still need human review before manuscript or thesis submission.

use_cases.title: Build a Thesis Bibliography
use_cases.description: Collect papers from PubMed and DOI lists, then produce a consistent BibTeX file for a thesis.

use_cases.title: Audit Manuscript References
use_cases.description: Check a manuscript bibliography for missing fields, duplicate keys, DOI problems, and inconsistent page formatting.

use_cases.title: Prepare Literature Review Sources
use_cases.description: Search scholarly databases, export candidate papers, and normalize metadata for review screening.

prompt_templates.title: Convert One DOI
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the citation-management skill to convert this DOI into a clean BibTeX entry: [DOI]. Explain any missing fields.

prompt_templates.title: Search PubMed Topic
prompt_templates.prompt: Search PubMed for papers about [topic] from [start year] to [end year]. Return the strongest references and note why each is relevant.

prompt_templates.title: Validate Reference File
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my BibTeX file for missing fields, duplicate entries, DOI problems, and formatting inconsistencies. Summarize required fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Build Search Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a citation workflow for [research question]. Include PubMed queries, DOI metadata extraction, deduplication, validation, and final BibTeX cleanup.

output_examples.input: A DOI for a Nature paper and a request for BibTeX.
output_examples.output: A formatted article citation with title, authors, journal, year, DOI, and a note about fields that could not be verified.

output_examples.input: A BibTeX file with duplicate keys and inconsistent page ranges.
output: Page ranges normalized to BibTeX style.
Remaining items marked for manual review.

output_examples.input: A PubMed query for recent clinical trial reviews.
output_examples.output: A ranked list of matching papers with PMIDs, journal names, publication years, and export guidance.

best_practices: Review all generated citations against publisher or database records before submission.
Use small result limits first to confirm search quality before running larger batches.
Store API keys in environment variables and avoid placing them in shared prompts or files.
anti_patterns: Do not assume Google Scholar results are complete or stable across repeated searches.
Do not run formatting scripts on your only copy of a bibliography without a backup.
Do not publish generated references without checking author names, titles, dates, and DOI resolution.

faq.question: Can this skill create BibTeX entries from DOIs?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes DOI conversion and broader metadata extraction for DOI, PMID, arXiv ID, and URL inputs.

faq.question: Does it need internet access?
faq.answer: Yes for search, DOI resolution, PubMed lookup, CrossRef lookup, arXiv lookup, and validation against external services.

faq.question: Does it store API keys?
faq.answer: No evidence found that it stores keys. It can read optional NCBI values from arguments or environment variables.

faq.question: Can it validate an existing BibTeX file?
faq.answer: Yes. It can check required fields, duplicate entries, DOI resolution, and common formatting problems.

faq.question: Will it work for non-biomedical research?
faq.answer: Yes for DOI, arXiv, CrossRef, Google Scholar, and BibTeX workflows. PubMed is focused on biomedical literature.

faq.question: Should I trust the output without review?
faq.answer: No. External metadata can be incomplete or inconsistent, so final citations need human review.
